scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes a specific temporary constitutional arrangement during the oligarchic coup of 411 BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This rule was enacted during the short-lived regime of the Four Hundred in 411 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the transition to the regime of the Five Thousand in late 411 BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The restriction of the franchise to five thousand citizens occurred during the political upheaval of 411 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle details the specific age requirements for the council established by the oligarchs in 411 BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The age qualification for the council was part of the revolutionary constitution of 411 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle evaluates the moderate oligarchy of 411/410 BCE which limited rights to those with hoplite equipment.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The hoplite franchise restriction was a brief institutional phase beginning in late 411 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This describes the economic policy of the moderate oligarchic regime following the Four Hundred.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The abolition of pay for all public offices was a key measure of the 411 BCE revolution.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle discusses legal changes made specifically by the Thirty Tyrants to existing Solonian laws.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Thirty Tyrants implemented these legislative repeals during their rule in 404 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This refers to the extra-legal violence and policy of confiscation enacted by the Thirty in 404/3 BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The targeted execution of wealthy citizens occurred during the reign of the Thirty in 404 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the specific 'Two Laws' the Thirty forced upon the Council to clarify who could be killed without trial.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: These enabling laws for summary execution were passed under the Thirty in 404 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This describes a specific retrospective disqualification law used by the Thirty against democratic sympathizers.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The disqualification for anti-oligarchic acts was part of the purge of 404 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle recounts the disarmament of the majority of the population by the Thirty.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Total disarmament of non-listed citizens happened in 404 BCE after the removal of Theramenes.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This refers to the unique, temporary independent polity at Eleusis created for sympathizers of the Thirty.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The terms of reconciliation establishing this independent government were settled in 403 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the specific restrictions the main Athenian state placed on the seceders at Eleusis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The exclusion of Eleusinian residents from Athenian office was part of the 403 BCE peace treaty.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle cites this legal battle to demonstrate the standard Athenian exclusion of slaves from political life.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The prosecution of Thrasybulus for trying to enfranchise slaves occurred in 403 BCE upon the democratic restoration.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the contemporaneous system of pay for attendance at the Assembly in his own day.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes this as the current practice during the writing of the Constitution of the Athenians (~330 BCE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the citizenship and enrollment laws as they functioned in his own time (4th century).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The double-parentage rule and deme enrollment procedures are contemporary to the author's floruit in the 330s BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This details the tax immunities for the Ephebi (military recruits) in 4th-century Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Ephebic system and its associated privileges are described as contemporary norms by Aristotle (~330 BCE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle explains the legal protections for young men in state service during his own era.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The immunity from litigation is presented as a functional rule of the contemporary 4th-century Athenian state.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the current procedural organization of the Athenian Ecclesia.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The right of petition in the Assembly is a component of the democratic system Aristotle observed (~330 BCE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle recounts a historical legal reform that became a permanent protection for citizens against the Council.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Although established earlier, Aristotle describes this as the current legal limit on the Council's power in his day.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the contemporary right of individual citizens to prosecute magistrates.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The right to impeach magistrates is listed as a feature of the contemporary 4th-century Athenian legal system.
